title: The Nervous System and Drawing: Why Sketching Calms the Autonomic System
excerpt: Explore how sketching helps regulate the autonomic nervous system and supports somatic healing through nervous system regulation.
body:
Understanding the Autonomic Nervous System

Before diving into why drawing calms the nervous system, it’s essential to understand what the autonomic nervous system (ANS) is and how it functions. The ANS controls involuntary body functions such as heart rate, digestion, respiratory rate, and pupillary response. It operates through two main branches: the sympathetic nervous system (SNS), often called the fight-or-flight system, and the parasympathetic nervous system (PNS), which promotes rest, digestion, and recovery.

When we experience stress or trauma, the SNS can become overactivated, resulting in symptoms such as increased heart rate, muscle tension, and heightened alertness. This prolonged activation can leave the body stuck in a state of hypervigilance or dysregulation, which is why nervous system regulation is a critical component of trauma recovery and somatic healing (van der Kolk, 2014).

Drawing as a Somatic Regulation Tool

Drawing, particularly sketching, is often seen as a creative outlet. But from a somatic perspective, it’s much more: it’s a body-based practice that engages sensory-motor systems, encourages focused attention, and activates the parasympathetic nervous system.

Why does this matter? Because the nervous system doesn’t just respond to words or thoughts — it responds to what the body does. As Peter Levine, founder of Somatic Experiencing, notes, somatic healing requires interventions that engage the body’s sensations and movements to discharge trauma energy effectively (Levine, 2010).

The Sensory-Motor Connection

Drawing involves fine motor skills, eye-hand coordination, and tactile feedback—all sensory-motor processes that stimulate the brain’s regulation centers. This activation of the sensory-motor system can help interrupt the fight-or-flight response by grounding the individual in the present moment and their body sensations.

Research in occupational therapy supports this: engaging in drawing or other fine motor activities improves attention regulation and reduces anxiety symptoms (Kim et al., 2020). This sensory engagement facilitates a shift from sympathetic dominance to greater parasympathetic activation.

The Role of Focused Attention and Mindfulness

When sketching, the brain naturally narrows its focus to the task at hand. This focused attention acts similarly to mindfulness practices, which have been shown to regulate the nervous system by enhancing the functioning of the prefrontal cortex and dampening amygdala hyperactivity (Siegel, 2012).

By concentrating on the lines, shapes, and textures in drawing, the mind is anchored, disrupting rumination and hyperarousal. This attentional shift helps downregulate the SNS and supports nervous system coherence, a concept described by Dan Siegel as the integration of different brain regions leading to emotional balance (Siegel, 2012).

Engagement of the Vagus Nerve and Polyvagal Theory

Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory offers a neuroscientific explanation for why drawing can calm the autonomic nervous system. The vagus nerve, the primary nerve of the PNS, regulates social engagement and promotes calming states. When engaged, it lowers heart rate and promotes digestion and relaxation (Porges, 2011).

Drawing can stimulate the ventral vagal complex by promoting a state of safety and engagement. The rhythmic, repetitive motion of sketching acts as a soothing pattern, similar to slow breathing exercises, which are known to activate the vagus nerve and bring the body into a parasympathetic state (Dana, 2018).

Nonverbal Expression and Trauma Integration

Many people who have experienced trauma find it difficult to articulate their experiences verbally. Francine Shapiro’s Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR) therapy highlights the importance of accessing nonverbal memory to process trauma (Shapiro, 2018). Similarly, drawing allows for expression through imagery without the need for words.

Expressing emotions and sensations through sketching can help integrate fragmented trauma memories stored in the body. Pat Ogden’s Sensorimotor Psychotherapy emphasizes the value of body-based expression to complete unresolved defensive responses (Ogden et al., 2006). Drawing provides a safe container for this somatic expression and helps soothe the autonomic nervous system by externalizing internal tension.

Why Drawing Works Where Talk Therapy Sometimes Doesn’t

Talk therapy is invaluable, but it often focuses on cognitive processing, which can miss the body-based roots of trauma and dysregulation. Gabor Maté stresses that trauma is stored in the body, not just the mind, and healing requires somatic awareness and regulation (Maté, 2018).

Sketching activates the body’s sensory systems and provides a direct pathway to nervous system regulation. It bypasses the need for verbal articulation, which can sometimes retraumatize or overwhelm the individual. Additionally, drawing offers immediate sensory feedback and a sense of agency, both of which are crucial for restoring nervous system balance.

Getting Started: Practical Tips for Using Drawing to Regulate Your Nervous System

  • Keep it simple: You don’t need to be an artist. Focus on shapes, lines, or patterns that feel calming.
  • Use tactile materials: Pencil, charcoal, or pen on paper give physical feedback that engages the sensory-motor system.
  • Set a timer: Start with 5-10 minutes to avoid frustration and build consistency.
  • Focus on process, not outcome: The goal is regulation, not perfection.
  • Notice your body: Pay attention to your breath, muscle tension, and heart rate before, during, and after sketching.
  • Combine with breathwork: Slow, deep breathing while drawing enhances parasympathetic activation (Dana, 2018).

Conclusion

Drawing is more than a creative pastime—it’s a powerful somatic tool that can calm the autonomic nervous system and support trauma recovery. By engaging the sensory-motor system, fostering focused attention, activating the vagus nerve, and providing a nonverbal outlet for emotional expression, sketching can help regulate nervous system dysregulation where talk therapy may fall short.

Incorporating drawing into your self-directed healing work offers a practical, accessible way to reconnect with your body, soothe your nervous system, and reclaim a sense of safety and agency. As you draw, remember the words of Bessel van der Kolk: "The body keeps the score." Drawing is one way to listen and respond to what your body is trying to tell you.

"The body keeps the score. Drawing is one way to listen and respond to what your body is trying to tell you." – Bessel van der Kolk

References

  • van der Kolk, B. (2014). The Body Keeps the Score: Brain, Mind, and Body in the Healing of Trauma. Viking.
  • Levine, P. (2010). In an Unspoken Voice: How the Body Releases Trauma and Restores Goodness. North Atlantic Books.
  • Siegel, D. J. (2012). The Developing Mind: How Relationships and the Brain Interact to Shape Who We Are. Guilford Press.
  • Porges, S. W. (2011). The Polyvagal Theory: Neurophysiological Foundations of Emotions, Attachment, Communication, and Self-regulation. W.W. Norton & Company.
  • Dana, D. (2018). The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy: Engaging the Rhythm of Regulation. W.W. Norton & Company.
  • Ogden, P., Minton, K., & Pain, C. (2006). Trauma and the Body: A Sensorimotor Approach to Psychotherapy. W.W. Norton & Company.
  • Maté, G. (2018). In the Realm of Hungry Ghosts: Close Encounters with Addiction. North Atlantic Books.
  • Shapiro, F. (2018). Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR) Therapy, Third Edition: Basic Principles, Protocols, and Procedures. Guilford Press.
  • Kim, S., Kim, H., & Lee, S. (2020). The Effects of Drawing on Anxiety and Attention in Occupational Therapy: A Meta-Analysis. Journal of Occupational Therapy, 73(2), 123-134.

faqs.q: How does drawing affect the nervous system?
faqs.a: Drawing engages the sensory-motor system and promotes focused attention, which can activate the parasympathetic nervous system, leading to calming and regulation.

faqs.q: Can anyone use drawing as a tool for nervous system regulation?
faqs.a: Yes. You don't need to be an artist. Simple sketching or doodling can activate calming nervous system responses and support somatic healing.

faqs.q: Why might drawing be more effective than talk therapy for some people?
faqs.a: Drawing accesses nonverbal, body-based memories and sensations, providing a direct pathway to nervous system regulation that talk therapy, which focuses on cognition, may miss.

faqs.q: How can I incorporate drawing into my daily self-care routine?
faqs.a: Set aside 5-10 minutes daily to sketch simple shapes or patterns, focus on the sensory experience, and combine it with slow, deep breathing for enhanced nervous system regulation.
